symptom: Cannot attach data disk to VM: error 'AttachDiskWhileBeingDetached' — the disk is currently being detached or the last detach operation failed
rootCause: Previous detach operation on the data disk failed, leaving the disk stuck in a 'being detached' state. The VM's StorageProfile still holds a reference to the disk.
solution: Use PowerShell: set $vm.StorageProfile.DataDisks[N].ToBeDetached = $true then Update-AzVM. Or use REST API PATCH with toBeDetached: true. API version must be 2019-03-01+.

symptom: Slow Azure disk attach/detach operations on AKS — takes much longer than expected when >10 operations on a single node or >3 on a VMSS pool
rootCause: The in-tree Azure disk driver (kubernetes.io/azure-disk) performs attach/detach operations sequentially, not in parallel. Deprecated since AKS 1.21.
solution: Migrate from in-tree driver to CSI driver. Check storage class provisioner — if 'kubernetes.io/azure-disk', migrate to disk.csi.azure.com. See docs: csi-migrate-in-tree-volumes.

symptom: Linux VM shuts down unexpectedly and fails to boot when OS disk is full. Serial console shows 'Starting Security Auditing Service' then immediately 'Power down'.
rootCause: auditd is configured with disk_full_action=HALT / admin_space_left_action=HALT in /etc/audit/auditd.conf. When the OS disk fills up, auditd triggers immediate system halt.
solution: 1) Access OS disk via az vm repair or recovery VM. 2) Change auditd.conf settings from HALT to SUSPEND/IGNORE. 3) Swap disk back and boot. 4) Free disk space (target 10% free). 5) Revert auditd.conf to original values. Automated fix available via ALAR tool 'auditd' action.

symptom: Linux VM doesn't fully boot after OS disk resize in Azure. Serial console shows '[Errno 28] No space left on device' from cloud-init.
rootCause: The OS disk was full before resize. Cloud-init tries to auto-resize the root filesystem on reboot but needs some free space to operate. With no free space, the resize operation fails and cloud-init can't complete.
solution: Clear unneeded data from the OS disk to free a small amount of space, then expand the filesystem. Use az vm repair or recovery VM to access the disk offline. Run resize2fs (ext4) or xfs_growfs (xfs) after freeing space.

symptom: Cannot resize MBR partition beyond 2 TB using fdisk on Linux VM data disk. fdisk shows 'DOS partition table format can not be used on drives for volumes larger than 2 TB'.
rootCause: MBR partition table uses 32-bit sector addressing, limiting partition size to 2^32 × 512 bytes = 2 TB for 512-byte sector disks. fdisk cannot address beyond this limit.
solution: Convert partition from MBR to GPT using gdisk: 1) Take snapshot. 2) Stop app and unmount. 3) Use gdisk to delete old partition and create new GPT partition with same start sector. 4) Verify GPT with 'gdisk -l'. 5) Run fsck then resize2fs/xfs_growfs. Only applicable to data disks — never convert OS disk to GPT (will cause no-boot).

symptom: Disk performance degradation: high latency, slow IO. VM Cached/Uncached IOPS Consumed Percentage or Data Disk IOPS/Bandwidth Consumed Percentage >= 95%.
rootCause: Workload exceeds the IOPS or throughput limits of the VM size (VM-level throttling) or the provisioned disk tier (disk-level throttling). Each VM size has cached and uncached IOPS/bandwidth caps; each disk SKU has its own IOPS/throughput limit.
solution: For VM-level throttling: resize VM to a larger size with higher IOPS/bandwidth caps (e.g., memory-optimized Ebdsv5/M-series). For disk-level throttling: enable read caching if available, upgrade disk tier (Standard→Premium→Ultra), or stripe multiple disks. Use PerfInsights for root cause analysis. Check Azure Metrics: VM Cached IOPS %, Data Disk IOPS %, Bandwidth %.

symptom: Storage account throttling for unmanaged disks: ThrottlingError in storage metrics, slow disk IO, high E2E latency. TotalRequests approaching 20000 per storage account.
rootCause: Standard storage accounts have a cumulative limit of 20000 IOPS. Individual VHDs are limited to 500 IOPS / 60 MBits. When total requests across all VHDs in one account exceed these limits, throttling occurs.
solution: Re-balance VHDs across multiple storage accounts. Migrate to managed disks (no per-account IOPS limit). For latency-sensitive workloads, migrate to Premium storage with DS/GS series VMs. Monitor ThrottlingError, TotalRequests, AverageE2ELatency, AverageServerLatency metrics.

symptom: VMs with customer-managed key (CMK) encrypted disks auto-shutdown. Disk I/O (read/write) fails approximately 1 hour after the key is disabled, deleted, or expired.
rootCause: When a customer-managed key in Azure Key Vault is disabled, deleted, or expired, managed disks can no longer unwrap the data encryption key (DEK). VMs automatically shut down and won't boot until the key issue is resolved.
solution: Re-enable the key in Key Vault, restore deleted key (if soft-delete/purge protection enabled), or assign a new key to the DiskEncryptionSet. Enable auto-key-rotation to prevent future expiry issues. Ensure Key Vault has purge protection enabled. Note: moving subscription between AAD tenants breaks CMK association.

symptom: Error 'Disk resizing is allowed only when creating a VM or when the VM is deallocated' when trying to expand a managed disk. Or disk resize appears successful but OS doesn't see the new size.
rootCause: Disk resize requires VM to be deallocated unless the disk meets 'Expand without downtime' requirements. After Azure-side resize, the OS partition/filesystem must also be expanded. The correct size may take up to 10 minutes to reflect.
solution: 1) Check if disk supports online expansion (Expand without downtime). 2) If not, Stop/Deallocate VM first. 3) Resize disk via portal/CLI/PowerShell. 4) Start VM. 5) Expand partition inside OS: Windows (Resize-Partition), Linux (resize2fs/xfs_growfs). 6) If size not reflected, perform disk rescan or reboot. Shrinking disks is NOT supported.

symptom: Error 'AcquireDiskLeaseFailed': Failed to acquire lease while creating disk using blob with URI. Blob is already in use. Or 'DiskBlobAlreadyInUseByAnotherDisk': Blob is already in use by another disk belonging to another VM.
rootCause: The VHD blob in storage has an active lease held by another disk/VM. Cannot create a new disk from a blob that is already attached. Common when manually managing unmanaged disks.
solution: Examine the blob metadata for disk reference information to identify which VM owns the disk. Detach or delete the conflicting disk/VM association first. Consider migrating to managed disks which handle lease management automatically.

symptom: Azure Disk Backup fails with errors like 'UserErrorNotEnoughPermissionOnDisk', 'UserErrorNotEnoughPermissionOnSnapshotRG', or 'UserErrorDiskBackupAuthorizationFailed'. Backup vault managed identity missing permissions.
rootCause: The Backup vault's managed identity lacks the required RBAC permissions on the source disk, snapshot data store resource group, or disk encryption set (DES). Common after initial backup configuration or when resources are moved.
solution: Grant the Backup vault managed identity: 1) Disk Backup Reader on the source disk. 2) Disk Snapshot Contributor on the snapshot data store RG. 3) Reader on the DES if using CMK encryption. For restore: grant Disk Restore Operator on the target RG. See Azure Backup RBAC requirements documentation.

symptom: Azure Disk Backup restore fails with 'UserErrorDiskSnapshotNotFound' or 'UserErrorSnapshotMetadataNotFound'. The disk snapshot for the restore point is not accessible or metadata is deleted.
rootCause: The snapshot associated with the selected restore point was deleted or moved from the snapshot data store resource group. Snapshot metadata may also be lost if the snapshot RG was recreated or had resource locks removed.
solution: Choose a different recovery point that has an intact snapshot. Follow recommended guidelines for snapshot RG management: do not delete snapshots manually, do not apply delete/read locks on the snapshot RG after backup is configured, ensure snapshot RG exists before scheduling backups.

symptom: Cannot resize (expand) a shared disk while it is attached to multiple VMs. Error or operation blocked when trying to expand a shared managed disk.
rootCause: Azure shared disks (maxShares > 1) cannot be expanded without either deallocating ALL VMs the disk is attached to, or detaching the disk from ALL VMs first. This is a platform limitation.
solution: To resize a shared disk: 1) Stop/deallocate ALL VMs attached to the shared disk, OR detach the disk from all VMs. 2) Resize the disk via portal/CLI/PowerShell. 3) Restart VMs / reattach disk. 4) Expand partition/filesystem inside each VM's OS. Plan for maintenance window as all cluster nodes will be down.

symptom: Shared Premium SSD (maxShares > 1) has no host caching and no disk bursting. Performance may be lower than expected compared to non-shared Premium SSD. Also Azure Disk Encryption (ADE) is not supported — only server-side encryption (SSE).
rootCause: Platform limitations for shared disks: host caching is disabled when maxShares > 1 (applies to all shared disk types). Disk bursting is also disabled for shared Premium SSDs. ADE is unsupported; only SSE is supported. These are by-design constraints.
solution: For performance: consider using Ultra Disk or Premium SSD v2 as shared disks (they don't rely on host caching and offer configurable IOPS/throughput). For encryption: use server-side encryption with platform-managed or customer-managed keys instead of ADE. For bursting needs: use non-shared disks or upgrade to higher tier.

symptom: Cannot use Ultra Disk or Premium SSD v2 as OS disk. Deployment fails when attempting to create a VM with Ultra Disk or Premium SSD v2 as OS disk.
rootCause: Ultra Disks and Premium SSD v2 can only be used as data disks. They cannot be used as OS disks. This is a platform limitation by design. They also cannot be used with Azure Compute Gallery.
solution: Use Premium SSD (v1) or Standard SSD as OS disk. Attach Ultra Disk or Premium SSD v2 as data disks only. For high-performance OS disk needs, use Premium SSD P-series with appropriate tier or enable performance plus (for P30+).

symptom: Ultra Disk deployment on VMSS with Uniform orchestration fails. Setting IOPS or throughput values that exceed the deployment-time formulas causes deployment failure.
rootCause: During deployment of Ultra Disks with Uniform VMSS, different (stricter) formulas apply: max IOPS = 300 IOPS/GiB (up to 400K), min throughput = 4KB/s per provisioned IOPS. Exceeding these limits at deployment time causes failure. After successful deployment, performance can be increased beyond these limits.
solution: During initial VMSS Uniform deployment, set conservative IOPS/throughput within the deployment formulas (max 300 IOPS/GiB, throughput = IOPS × 4KB/s min). After deployment succeeds, use 'az disk update' or Update-AzDisk to increase performance up to the regular Ultra Disk limits (1000 IOPS/GiB). Performance changes take up to 1 hour and are limited to 4 times per 24h.

symptom: Disk performance drops to baseline after ~30 minutes of high IO. Credit-based bursting credits exhausted. Burst metrics show 'Used Burst IO Credits Percentage' at 100%.
rootCause: Credit-based bursting (enabled by default on Premium SSD ≤512 GiB and Standard SSD ≤1024 GiB) allows max 30 minutes of burst at peak rate. Once credits are depleted, performance falls back to the provisioned baseline until credits reaccumulate. On-demand bursting (Premium SSD >512 GiB only) has no time limit but incurs extra charges.
solution: 1) For sustained high IO: upgrade disk tier (e.g., P20→P30) for higher baseline IOPS. 2) For Premium SSD >512 GiB: enable on-demand bursting (note: can only be enabled when disk is detached or VM is stopped). 3) Change performance tier temporarily for burst-heavy periods. 4) Monitor 'Used Burst IO Credits Percentage' metric to track credit consumption.

symptom: Cannot enable Performance Plus on an existing disk. Feature is greyed out or API returns error. Performance Plus only available during disk creation.
rootCause: Performance Plus (which increases IOPS/throughput limits for Premium SSD, Standard SSD, and Standard HDD ≥513 GiB) can only be enabled at disk creation time. It cannot be enabled on existing disks or disks created during VM creation. Not supported for disks recovered with Azure Site Recovery.
solution: Workaround: 1) Create a snapshot of the existing disk. 2) Create a new disk from the snapshot with Performance Plus enabled (--performance-plus true). 3) Swap the old disk for the new disk on the VM. Note: disk must be ≥513 GiB. Cannot be enabled during VM creation — create the disk separately first, then attach to VM.

symptom: AKS pod stuck in ContainerCreating: 'Disk cannot be attached to the VM because it is not in the same zone as the VM'. Azure disk in availability zone X but pod scheduled on node in zone Y.
rootCause: Default AKS storage classes for Azure disks use LRS (locally redundant storage), which deploys disks in a specific availability zone. When AKS schedules a pod to a node in a different zone than the disk, the attach operation fails with BadRequest.
solution: 1) Use nodeAffinity with topology.disk.csi.azure.com/zone to pin pod to same zone as disk. 2) Use ZRS (zone-redundant storage) disks: create StorageClass with Premium_ZRS or StandardSSD_ZRS — ZRS disks attach to any zone. 3) Use Azure Files (NFS/SMB) instead of Azure Disk for cross-zone access.

symptom: AKS pod stuck in ContainerCreating: 'AuthorizationFailed — client does not have authorization to perform action Microsoft.Compute/disks/read over scope'. AKS identity cannot access disk in external resource group.
rootCause: AKS cluster's managed identity lacks RBAC permissions on the Azure disk resource. This occurs when the disk is created in a resource group other than the AKS infrastructure (MC_*) resource group.
solution: Assign Contributor role to AKS control plane identity on the disk or its resource group: az role assignment create --assignee <AKS-identity-ID> --role 'Contributor' --scope <disk-resource-id>. Use the control plane managed identity (not kubelet identity) for disk management.

symptom: AKS pod stuck in ContainerCreating: 'Multi-Attach error for volume — Volume is already used by pod(s)'. Azure disk cannot be mounted to pods on different nodes simultaneously.
rootCause: Azure managed disks can only be mounted as ReadWriteOnce (RWO), meaning the disk attaches to a single node only. If a pod on another node tries to mount the same PVC/disk, the attach fails because the disk is already attached elsewhere.
solution: 1) Ensure the disk is not mounted by multiple pods on different nodes — reschedule pods to same node or use pod anti-affinity. 2) For cross-node shared storage, use Azure Files (NFS/SMB) instead of Azure Disk. 3) For shared block storage, use Azure shared disks with maxShares > 1 (requires SCSI reservation-aware application like WSFC/Pacemaker).

symptom: AKS pod fails to mount Ultra Disk: 'StorageAccountType UltraSSD_LRS can be used only when additionalCapabilities.ultraSSDEnabled is set'. Ultra Disk attach rejected by node pool.
rootCause: Ultra Disk support is disabled by default on AKS node pools. The VMSS backing the node pool does not have additionalCapabilities.ultraSSDEnabled set, so attaching an Ultra Disk PV fails with InvalidParameter.
solution: Create a new AKS node pool with Ultra Disk support enabled: az aks nodepool add --enable-ultra-ssd. Ultra Disks cannot be enabled on existing node pools — a new node pool must be created. Schedule pods using Ultra Disk PVs to the Ultra Disk-enabled node pool via nodeSelector or nodeAffinity.

symptom: AKS pod fails to mount disk: 'applyFSGroup failed for vol — input/output error'. Pod startup hangs or fails when mounting PVC with securityContext.fsGroup set on a volume containing many files.
rootCause: Kubernetes by default recursively changes ownership and permissions (chown/chmod) for all files in a volume to match the fsGroup specified in the pod's securityContext. For volumes with many files/directories, this recursive operation takes excessive time and can trigger I/O errors or timeout.
solution: Set fsGroupChangePolicy: 'OnRootMismatch' in the pod's securityContext. This only changes permissions if the root directory ownership doesn't match, drastically reducing mount time. Example: spec.securityContext.fsGroupChangePolicy: 'OnRootMismatch'. Requires Kubernetes 1.20+.

symptom: AKS pod stuck in Pending: 'FailedScheduling — 0/N nodes are available: N node(s) exceed max volume count'. No node in the cluster can accept additional disk attachments.
rootCause: Each Azure VM size has a maximum number of data disks it can attach. When all nodes in the cluster have reached their disk attachment limit, new pods requiring Azure disk PVs cannot be scheduled. The limit depends on VM size (e.g., Standard_D2s_v3 supports max 4 data disks).
solution: 1) Add a new node pool with a VM size that supports more disks. 2) Scale out the existing node pool to add nodes with available disk capacity. 3) Delete unused PVCs/disks from existing nodes. 4) Check Kubernetes default volume limits per node and ensure disk count doesn't exceed them.

symptom: Unexpected high disk read latency (lasting hours to ~24h) after performing control plane operations on managed disks — such as changing storage type, detach/attach between VMs, creating disk from VHD/snapshot, or converting unmanaged to managed disks.
rootCause: Control plane operations may trigger background data copy that moves the disk between storage locations. During the copy (up to 24h depending on data size), reads can be redirected to the original location, increasing latency. For Premium SSD v2 / Ultra Disks with 4K sector size: higher read latency. With 512e sector size: higher read AND write latency.
solution: Plan control plane operations during maintenance windows. No way to avoid the background copy — it's platform behavior. Monitor disk latency metrics after operations. Expect latency normalization within 24 hours. For latency-sensitive workloads on Ultra/PremiumV2, prefer 4K sector size (read-only latency impact vs both read+write for 512e).

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xc00000ba: 'The operating system couldn't be loaded because a critical system driver is missing or contains errors'. File path shown: \Windows\System32\drivers\<filename>.
rootCause: Windows system files are corrupt. Common causes: unfinished Windows update/installation, incomplete file deletion, malware infection, improper shutdown, or bad application uninstall leaving orphaned/damaged driver files.
solution: 1) Attach OS disk to recovery VM as data disk. 2) Run DISM with WinRE as source: Dism /image:G:\ /cleanup-image /restorehealth /source:wim:G:\recovery\windowsRE\winre.wim:1. 3) If WinRE lacks the binary, download full Windows ISO and use its WIM. 4) Run SFC: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=G:\ /offwindir=G:\windows. 5) Detach disk (wait 3 min for lease release) and recreate VM. 6) If caused by recent software install, load SYSTEM hive in regedit, set service Start=4 to disable it.

symptom: Windows VM with Azure Disk Encryption doesn't start. Boot diagnostics shows BitLocker recovery prompts: 'Plug in USB driver with BitLocker key', 'You are locked out! Enter the recovery key', or 'Enter the password to unlock this drive'.
rootCause: The VM cannot locate the BitLocker Recovery Key (BEK) file to decrypt the encrypted OS disk. This typically occurs after maintenance events, VM migrations, or Key Vault access issues that prevent automatic BEK retrieval.
solution: 1) Stop and deallocate the VM, then restart to force BEK re-retrieval from Azure Key Vault. 2) If that fails, attach the encrypted OS disk to a recovery VM, install Az PowerShell, download BEK from Key Vault using Get-AzKeyVaultSecret, unlock with manage-bde -unlock. 3) For KEK (Wrapped BEK) scenarios, use the unwrapkey REST API to extract BEK first. 4) After unlocking, detach disk and recreate VM or Swap OS disk.

symptom: Enabling Azure Disk Encryption on Windows VM fails with error: 'Azure Disk Encryption extension version 2.2 is not supported' (ErrorCode: NotSupported, StatusCode: 409 Conflict).
rootCause: The subscription does not support ADE extension version 2.2 without the UnifiedDiskEncryptionForVMs feature flag registered.
solution: Method 1: Revert to using Microsoft Entra (AAD) parameters (-AadClientID, -AadClientSecret) in Set-AzVMDiskEncryptionExtension. Method 2: Register UnifiedDiskEncryptionForVMs feature: Register-AzProviderFeature -ProviderNamespace Microsoft.Compute -FeatureName UnifiedDiskEncryptionForVMs, wait ~10 min, then Register-AzResourceProvider -ProviderNamespace Microsoft.Compute.

symptom: Azure Disk Encryption fails with error 'Failed to send DiskEncryptionData...' when trying to encrypt a VM.
rootCause: Common causes: 1) Key Vault in different region/subscription than VM, 2) Key Vault advanced access policies not configured for ADE, 3) KEK disabled or deleted, 4) Typo in Key Vault/KEK resource ID or URL, 5) Special characters in VM/disk/key names, 6) Unsupported encryption scenario, 7) Network issues blocking VM-to-Key-Vault connectivity.
solution: 1) Ensure Key Vault same region/subscription as VM. 2) Set Key Vault advanced access policies for disk encryption. 3) Verify KEK exists and is enabled. 4) Check for typos in disk-encryption-keyvault (full resource ID) and key-encryption-key (full URI). 5) Ensure names follow naming restrictions. 6) Verify no ADE restrictions violated. 7) Meet network requirements.

symptom: Azure Disk Encryption fails behind a firewall/proxy/NSG with 'Extension status not available on the VM'. Encryption never finishes.
rootCause: Firewall, proxy, or NSG settings block the ADE extension from reaching required endpoints: Azure Key Vault, Microsoft Entra endpoints, Azure Instance Metadata Service (169.254.169.254), Azure platform IP (168.63.129.16), and Azure storage endpoints.
solution: 1) Ensure NSG/firewall allows outbound to Key Vault endpoints, Microsoft Entra endpoints (M365 URLs sections 56/59), and Azure storage. 2) Allow IMDS endpoint 169.254.169.254 and virtual public IP 168.63.129.16. 3) Do not alter HTTP traffic to these addresses with proxy. 4) Refer to ADE networking requirements documentation.

symptom: Azure Disk Encryption fails on Windows Server 2016 Server Core because bdehdcfg component is not available by default.
rootCause: Server Core installations do not include bdehdcfg.exe binary which ADE requires to split system volume from OS volume.
solution: Copy 4 files from Windows Server 2016 Data Center (full) VM: bdehdcfg.exe, bdehdcfglib.dll, en-US/bdehdcfglib.dll.mui, en-US/bdehdcfg.exe.mui to same paths on Server Core. Run bdehdcfg.exe -target default to create 550 MB system partition, restart, then proceed with ADE encryption.

symptom: Azure portal shows disk as encrypted even after it was manually decrypted inside the VM using manage-bde. VM may fail to provision properly.
rootCause: Using low-level commands (manage-bde) to decrypt disk inside VM bypasses ADE management layer. Platform-level encryption settings and extension settings remain unchanged, causing status mismatch.
solution: Always use ADE management commands: PowerShell Disable-AzVMDiskEncryption then Remove-AzVMDiskEncryptionExtension (in that order). CLI: az vm encryption disable. Never use manage-bde directly to decrypt ADE-encrypted disks.

symptom: Ultra Disk configured with high IOPS (e.g., 40,000) but VM only achieves much lower IOPS (e.g., 12,800). Disk not reaching provisioned performance.
rootCause: VM-level IO throttle limits total IOPS a VM can drive, independent of individual disk limits. The VM size determines max uncached IOPS. Even if disk supports higher IOPS, the VM caps throughput.
solution: 1) Check VM size uncached IOPS limit to ensure >= total IOPS needed across all disks. 2) Upgrade to larger VM size supporting required IOPS/throughput. 3) Use VM Uncached IOPS Consumed Percentage metric to confirm VM-level throttling (100% = bottleneck). 4) Check Data Disk IOPS Consumed Percentage per LUN for per-disk utilization.

symptom: Attempt to shrink or downsize a managed disk fails. No option available in portal, CLI, or PowerShell to reduce disk size.
rootCause: Azure Managed Disks do not support shrinking or downsizing. This is by design. Only increasing disk size is supported.
solution: Shrinking managed disks is not supported. Workaround: 1) Create a new smaller disk of desired size, 2) Attach both old and new disks to same VM, 3) Copy data from old to new disk, 4) Detach and delete old disk. For OS disks, use recovery VM approach.

symptom: Host caching (ReadOnly/ReadWrite) cannot be enabled for managed disks provisioned at 4096 GiB or larger. Disk caching option greyed out or API returns error.
rootCause: Azure host caching (BlobCache) only supported for disk sizes up to 4095 GiB. Disks provisioned >= 4096 GiB cannot use host caching. A P50 at 4095 GiB CAN use caching; a P50 at 4096 GiB cannot.
solution: 1) If host caching required, keep disk provisioned size <= 4095 GiB. 2) For disks >= 4096 GiB, set caching to None. 3) For performance on large disks without caching, consider Premium SSD v2 or Ultra Disk with higher baseline performance.

symptom: Cannot enable on-demand disk bursting on Premium SSD. Operation fails or option not available. Error when trying to enable while disk attached to running VM.
rootCause: On-demand bursting constraints: 1) Cannot enable on Premium SSD <= 512 GiB (always credit-based). 2) Only supported on Premium SSDs. 3) Can only enable when disk detached or VM stopped/deallocated. 4) Cannot disable within 12 hours of enabling. 5) Not auto-disabled when performance tier changes.
solution: 1) Ensure disk size > 512 GiB. 2) Stop/deallocate VM or detach disk before enabling. 3) Portal: Disk > Configuration > Enable on-demand bursting. CLI: az disk update --enable-bursting true. PowerShell: New-AzDiskUpdateConfig -BurstingEnabled $true | Update-AzDisk. 4) To disable, wait 12h after enabling. 5) Note: incurs additional charges (hourly flat fee + per-burst-transaction costs).

symptom: Cannot copy Ultra Disk or Premium SSD v2 instant access snapshot cross-region or download underlying data. Snapshot stuck in InstantAccess or Pending state.
rootCause: Ultra Disk and Premium SSD v2 instant access snapshots require background data copy to complete before cross-region copy or data download. Snapshot states: Pending (not usable), InstantAccess (can create disks only, no copy/download), Available (full ops), AvailableWithInstantAccess (both fast restore and full ops).
solution: 1) Check CompletionPercent property on snapshot -- wait until 100% for full availability. 2) Check SnapshotAccessState for current capabilities. 3) InstantAccessDurationMins must be 60-300 min. 4) Max 15 concurrent disk creations from all instant access snapshots per disk. 5) Cannot create instant access snapshot of disk being hydrated (disk CompletionPercent < 100).

symptom: Cannot delete VM restore point: error 'DiskRestorePointUsedByCustomer - There is an active shared access signature outstanding for disk restore point'. Delete operation blocked.
rootCause: Active Shared Access Signatures (SAS) exist on the underlying disk restore points. Azure prevents deletion while SAS tokens are outstanding.
solution: Call EndGetAccess on all disk restore points within the restore point collection to revoke the SAS tokens, then retry the delete operation.

symptom: Restore point creation fails with 'OperationNotAllowed - disk(s) have not been allocated successfully'. Cannot create VM restore point.
rootCause: One or more disks attached to the VM are not properly allocated (failed provisioning or incomplete attachment). Restore point requires all disks to be healthy.
solution: Exclude the unallocated disks using the excludeDisks property in REST API, or use CLI/PowerShell/Portal to exclude them before triggering restore point creation.

symptom: Restore point creation fails with 'DiskRestorePointClientError - Keyvault associated with DiskEncryptionSet not found'. VM has CMK-encrypted disks.
rootCause: The Key Vault used for the DiskEncryptionSet has been deleted or is inaccessible. Restore point creation needs encryption keys to create encrypted disk restore points.
solution: Re-create the missing Key Vault resource with the same name and restore the encryption keys, then retry the restore point operation.

symptom: Restore point creation fails: 'VMRestorePointClientError - Restore Point creation failed due to VSS Writers in bad state'. Application-consistent snapshot cannot be taken.
rootCause: VSS writers are in a bad/failed state, preventing the VM snapshot extension from flushing in-memory IOs to disk for application-consistent snapshots.
solution: 1) Run 'vssadmin list writers' to identify failed writers. 2) Restart the failed VSS writer services (net stop/start). 3) Restart the VM. 4) Retry restore point creation.

symptom: Restore point creation fails: 'VMRestorePointClientError - maximum allowed snapshot limit of one or more disk blobs has been reached'. Cannot create new restore points.
rootCause: Total restore points across restore point collections and resource groups for a VM exceeds the 500-snapshot limit per disk blob.
solution: Delete older/unnecessary restore points from existing restore point collections to bring the count below 500, then retry.

symptom: Azure VM encrypted with ADE - OS disk appears locked with padlock icon when attached to repair VM for offline troubleshooting. Cannot access the volume.
rootCause: Disk encrypted with BitLocker via ADE. When attached to a different VM as data disk, it remains locked until unlocked with BitLocker Encryption Key (BEK).
solution: For ADE v2 (single-pass, managed disk): Use 'az vm repair create' for automated unlock, or attach disk during repair VM creation so BEK volume is auto-fetched. Assign drive letter to BEK volume, find .bek file with 'dir H: /a:h /b /s', run 'manage-bde -unlock G: -RecoveryKey H:\{GUID}.BEK'. For ADE v1/unmanaged: retrieve BEK from Key Vault via PowerShell.

symptom: VM backup snapshot fails: 'ExtensionSnapshotBitlockerError - This drive is locked by BitLocker Drive Encryption'. VSS snapshot blocked.
rootCause: BitLocker Drive Encryption on VM drives blocks the VSS snapshot operation used by Azure Backup.
solution: Turn off BitLocker for all drives on the VM (or the problematic drives), verify VSS issue resolved, retry backup.

symptom: VM backup fails: 'UserErrorFsFreezeFailed - Failed to freeze one or more mount-points of the VM'. Linux VM backup failure.
rootCause: Backup extension cannot fsfreeze mount points. Causes: duplicate mount entries in fstab, corrupt filesystem, inaccessible mount points.
solution: 1) Unmount problematic devices (umount). 2) Run fsck. 3) Remount and retry. 4) If unmount fails, edit /etc/azure/vmbackup.conf: MountsToSkip = /mnt/resource. 5) Check for duplicate mount entries.

symptom: VM backup fails: 'ExtensionFailedSnapshotLimitReachedError - Snapshot operation failed as snapshot limit exceeded for some disks attached'.
rootCause: Blob snapshot count for attached disks exceeded max limit. Azure Site Recovery or soft-delete retention policies can accumulate too many snapshots.
solution: 1) Delete unnecessary disk blob-snapshots. 2) If soft-delete enabled, adjust retention. 3) If ASR enabled, set isanysnapshotfailed=false in /etc/azure/vmbackup.conf and schedule ASR at different time.

symptom: VSS shadow copy provider times out during backup: 'The shadow copy provider timed out while holding writes to the volume'. Backup fails due to high disk IO.
rootCause: Excessive IOPS activity on the disk during backup window causes VSS shadow copy provider to time out.
solution: 1) Distribute IO load across multiple VM disks. 2) Schedule backup during off-peak hours. 3) Upgrade to higher IOPS disk tier. 4) Enable diagnostic metrics to check IOPS throttling.

symptom: Data disk shows as Offline or Missing in Windows Disk Management inside Azure VM. No drive letter assigned. May show error icon or 'Not Initialized'.
rootCause: Multiple causes: disk not initialized (no valid signature), dynamic disk from another system shows Foreign, I/O errors/corruption, SAN policy set to offline for new disks, third-party software brought volumes offline.
solution: 1) Right-click offline disk -> Online. 2) If Not Initialized -> Initialize Disk (MBR/GPT). 3) If Foreign -> Import Foreign Disks. 4) Check SAN policy: diskpart -> san -> set 'san policy=OnlineAll'. 5) If no drive letter -> Change Drive Letter and Paths -> Add.

symptom: Cannot take data disk offline in standalone Windows Server 2022 VM. VM hangs when using Disk Management or DiskPart to offline the disk.
rootCause: Disk configuration issues, virtual storage subsystem/controller problems, or disk corruption causing system freeze when processing offline request.
solution: 1) Install pending WS2022 updates. 2) Run chkdsk /scan and review event logs. 3) If not OS disk, detach and attach to different VM to verify health. 4) Collect Get-Disk output and contact Microsoft Support if persists.

symptom: Shared disk cannot be expanded (resized) while attached to VMs. Resize operation fails or is blocked.
rootCause: Azure shared disks require all VMs to be deallocated or the disk to be detached from all VMs before resizing. Platform limitation for data integrity.
solution: Deallocate all VMs the shared disk is attached to OR detach the disk from all VMs, then resize, then reattach/restart.

symptom: Azure Disk Encryption (ADE/BitLocker/DM-Crypt) cannot be enabled on shared disks. Deployment fails.
rootCause: Azure Disk Encryption (guest-level) is not supported for shared disks (maxShares > 1). Only server-side encryption (SSE) is supported.
solution: Use server-side encryption (SSE) with platform-managed keys (default) or customer-managed keys via DiskEncryptionSet instead of ADE.

symptom: Azure Disk Backup restore fails with 'UserErrorNotEnoughPermissionOnTargetRG'. Restored disk cannot be created in target resource group.
rootCause: Backup vault managed identity lacks Disk Restore Operator role on target resource group.
solution: 1) Click 'Grant Permissions' on restore validation page. 2) Wait ~15 min for role propagation. 3) Revalidate and restore. 4) Revoke role after restore completes.

symptom: Disk restore fails: 'UserErrorSameNameDiskAlreadyExists'. Cannot restore because disk with same name exists in target resource group.
rootCause: A managed disk with the specified restore name already exists. Azure does not allow overwriting existing disks during restore.
solution: Provide a different name for the restored disk, or choose a different target resource group.

symptom: After VM restore from backup, disks appear offline or Linux VM missing folders/mount points. Restored disks not mounted correctly.
rootCause: Windows: restore script machine does not meet OS requirements or restoring to same source. Linux: disks mounted by device name (/dev/sdX) instead of UUID; device names reassigned randomly after restore.
solution: Windows: verify OS requirements, do not restore to same source. Linux: always mount via UUID from blkid in /etc/fstab instead of /dev/sdX device names.

symptom: Cannot extend encrypted OS volume in Windows VM: Extend Volume grayed out for C: drive. System Reserved partition placed after C: blocks extension.
rootCause: When ADE/BitLocker applied to custom image VM where C: is partition 1 (no pre-existing System Reserved), Azure creates System Reserved as partition 2, blocking C: extend.
solution: Resize OS disk SKU. Extend System Reserved into unallocated (leave 200MB). Create new boot volume, run bcdboot. Copy BCD, update bcdedit, mark active. Delete old System Reserved, extend C:.

symptom: Windows VM boot error INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE or Boot failure Reboot and Select proper Boot device.
rootCause: BCD corrupted or partition containing Windows not marked active (Gen1 only).
solution: Stop/deallocate restart. If persists: attach to repair VM, verify Active via diskpart, chkdsk /f, repair BCD with bcdedit. Gen2 use EFI partition path.

symptom: Windows VM boot error: A disk read error occurred. Press Ctrl+Alt+Del to restart.
rootCause: Disk structure corrupted and unreadable. Gen1: boot partition may not be Active.
solution: Create repair VM, attach OS disk. Set Active with diskpart (Gen1). chkdsk /f. Enable serial console. Rebuild VM.

symptom: Windows VM boot error: This is not a bootable disk. Please insert a bootable floppy.
rootCause: OS boot process cannot locate active system partition or missing BCD reference to Windows partition.
solution: Repair VM: set Active via diskpart, chkdsk /f, bcdboot to create boot record, rebuild VM.

symptom: Windows Boot Manager error 0xC0000225 Status not found: boot selection failed, required device inaccessible.
rootCause: BCD corrupted, or VHD migrated from on-premises not prepared correctly.
solution: Attach to troubleshooting VM. Identify Boot/Windows partitions. Repair BCD via bcdedit (set bootmgr/loader device). Gen2 use EFI path.

symptom: Cannot delete storage account/container/VHD blob: lease on blob/container, no lease ID specified. Deletion blocked.
rootCause: VHD page blob Lease State is Leased because attached to VM. Azure prevents deletion to avoid corruption.
solution: Identify attached VM via blob metadata MicrosoftAzureCompute_VMName. OS disk: delete VM. Data disk: detach. Wait for lease release then retry.

symptom: Disk type conversion rejected: can only change disk type twice per day.
rootCause: Azure enforces max 2 disk type changes per day per disk.
solution: Wait until next day for additional changes. Plan conversions to minimize changes.

symptom: Cannot directly convert to Premium SSD v2 from Gallery image disk or Ultra Disk. Multiple conversion restrictions.
rootCause: PSSv2 conversion limits: no OS disk, 512 sector only, max 50 concurrent/sub/region, must detach shared, disable caching/bursting/double-encryption, no Gallery image, no direct Ultra/PSSv2 to other type.
solution: Use incremental snapshot migration for Ultra/PSSv2 conversion. Create snapshot then new disk with target type. Ensure 512 logical sector size.

symptom: Write Accelerator cannot be enabled on non-M-series VM. Feature unavailable or deployment fails.
rootCause: Write Accelerator M-series only. Cache must be None/ReadOnly. Only IO<=64KiB accelerated. Snapshots not supported on WA OS disks.
solution: Verify M-series VM. Set cache None/ReadOnly. For non-M-series use Ultra Disk for low-latency writes.

symptom: Data loss with ReadWrite host caching on data disks when VM crashes. Cached writes not persisted.
rootCause: ReadWrite cache stores writes in host memory/SSD before Azure Storage. VM crash before flush loses cached data. OS disk None setting overridden to ReadOnly.
solution: Use ReadWrite only when app handles flushing (SQL Server). None for write-heavy. ReadOnly for read-heavy. Cache change detaches/reattaches disk.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after ADE: emergency mode, /dev/mapper/rootvg-rootlv does not exist. ADE modules missing from initramfs.
rootCause: Required ADE crypt-ade scripts not included in initramfs during encryption.
solution: Rescue VM chroot. RHEL: dracut -f --regenerate-all. Ubuntu: update-initramfs -u -k all. Verify with lsinitrd. Swap disk back.

symptom: Linux ADE encryption interrupted: VM emergency mode, LUKS header (osluksheader) missing/corrupted.
rootCause: ADE interrupted (extension failure/timeout). Partial encryption with missing LUKS header or ADE key in BEK volume.
solution: Restore from backup. If none: create test VM, encrypt data volume same settings, copy ADE key from BEK, chroot to manually open encrypted disk.

symptom: Linux ADE fails: required packages cannot install. Extension log shows package errors.
rootCause: VM lacks repo access or repo config broken. ADE needs cryptsetup, lvm2 etc.
solution: Ensure repo access. For isolated networks see ADE isolated network docs. Check extension.log for failed package.

symptom: Linux ADE boot failure: missing rd.luks.ade.partuuid/bootuuid in GRUB. Emergency mode.
rootCause: ADE extension failed to add UUID parameters to /etc/default/grub.
solution: Offline: rescue VM chroot, blkid for UUIDs, add rd.luks.ade.partuuid/bootuuid to GRUB, regenerate.

symptom: Ubuntu 14.04 ADE fails: OOM Killer terminates dd during OS encryption.
rootCause: Kernel 4.4 bug: OOM Killer improperly kills dd. Fixed in Azure-tuned kernel 4.15+.
solution: Update kernel: apt-get install linux-azure, reboot. Verify uname -a. Ensure VM agent >= 2.2.38.

symptom: Cannot disable ADE on Linux VM with encrypted OS disk (volumeType=ALL/OS).
rootCause: Azure does not support disabling ADE when Linux OS disk is encrypted. Only data-disk-only encryption can be disabled.
solution: Encrypted OS: restore from backup or redeploy. Data-only: Disable-AzVMDiskEncryption or az vm encryption disable --volume-type data.

symptom: Ephemeral OS disk data lost after stop/deallocate, reimage, or host maintenance. VM state reset.
rootCause: EOSD on local VM storage, not Azure Storage. Stop/deallocate or host healing recreates from image. No snapshots/backups/ADE.
solution: Design stateless workloads. Persist data externally. Do not stop/deallocate EOSD VMs.

symptom: Ephemeral OS disk deployment fails: VM size insufficient local cache/temp for OS image.
rootCause: VM local SSD cache or temp disk too small for OS image.
solution: Verify VM supports EOSD (EphemeralOSDiskSupported). Check cache/temp >= image. Use Temp Disk placement for AVD. Larger VM size.

symptom: Temporary disk data lost after VM maintenance, redeployment, or stop/resize.
rootCause: Temp disk on physical host, not Azure Storage. Lost during maintenance/redeployment/stop-deallocate.
solution: Never store persistent data on temp disk. Use for scratch only (page files, swap, tempdb). Use managed data disks for persistence.

symptom: ZRS managed disks higher write latency vs LRS. ZRS unavailable for Premium SSD v2 or Ultra Disk.
rootCause: ZRS replicates synchronously across 3 AZs adding latency. Only Premium SSD/Standard SSD. Force detach data disks only.
solution: Benchmark ZRS vs LRS. Use ZRS for HA. For Ultra/PSSv2 use LRS + cross-zone DR.

symptom: Cannot export/download managed disk VHD: AuthorizationFailed or blocked. NetworkAccessPolicy DenyAll.
rootCause: NetworkAccessPolicy DenyAll/AllowPrivate. beginGetAccess RBAC permission may be missing.
solution: Check NetworkAccessPolicy. DenyAll: change to AllowAll/AllowPrivate. AllowPrivate: create disk access + private endpoint. Ensure RBAC has beginGetAccess.

symptom: Disk access private endpoint: max 100 concurrent import/export. Upload fails with both disk access and encryption set.
rootCause: Disk access limits: 100 concurrent ops, cannot combine with disk encryption set for upload, same region/sub required.
solution: Use multiple disk access resources. Remove encryption set before upload via private endpoint. Same region/subscription.

symptom: Orphaned unattached managed disks incur costs after VM deletion. Unexpected disk charges.
rootCause: VM deletion does not delete attached disks by default. Disks become unattached but billable.
solution: Find: portal Disks filter Unattached. PS: Get-AzDisk Where ManagedBy null. CLI: az disk list query managedBy null. Delete unneeded. Azure Advisor.

symptom: OS disk swap: repaired disk not in Swap OS disk list for 10-15 min after detaching from repair VM.
rootCause: Lease release and metadata propagation takes 10-15 minutes after detach.
solution: Wait 10-15 min after detaching. Portal: VM > Disks > Swap OS disk. PowerShell: Set-AzVMOSDisk then Update-AzVM.

symptom: Data Box Disk drive gets mounted as read-only on Linux client during data copy
rootCause: Unclean NTFS filesystem on the Data Box Disk causes Linux to mount it as read-only. Remounting as read-write with dislocker doesn't persist data.
solution: Install ntfsprogs, unmount, run ntfsfix on the dislocker-file path, remove hibernation metadata with ntfs-3g -o remove_hiberfile, then do a clean unlock and remount.

symptom: Data copied to Data Box Disk does not persist after unmount and remount
rootCause: Drive was initially mounted as read-only due to unclean filesystem, then remounted as read-write, but data written in the remounted state does not persist.
solution: Follow the read-only drive resolution: ntfsfix + clean unmount/mount cycle. If issue persists, collect logs from Data Box Disk Unlock tool folder and contact Microsoft Support.

symptom: Data Box Disk Split Copy tool throws 'Sequence contains no elements' when retrieving BitLocker password
rootCause: Destination Data Box Disks are offline in Windows.
solution: Use diskmgmt.msc to bring the Data Box Disks online, then retry the Split Copy operation.

symptom: ERROR_BLOB_OR_FILE_SIZE_ALIGNMENT when copying data to Data Box page blob share or managed disk folder
rootCause: Files copied to the page blob share are not 512-byte aligned. Only VHD/VHDX files (512-byte aligned) are valid for page blob shares.
solution: Remove non-VHD/VHDX data from page blob share. Use block blob or Azure Files shares for generic data. For managed disks, ensure only fixed VHDs are uploaded.

symptom: ERROR_BLOB_OR_FILE_TYPE_UNSUPPORTED in Data Box managed disk share - only fixed VHDs allowed
rootCause: VHDX files, dynamic VHDs, or differencing VHDs were copied to managed disk folders. Only fixed VHDs are supported.
solution: Only upload fixed VHDs to managed disk folders (Premium SSD, Standard HDD, Standard SSD). Remove any VHDX, dynamic, or differencing VHD files.

symptom: Data Box REST API authentication fails with AuthenticationFailed error during data copy
rootCause: Device time is not synced with Azure, causing large time skew that breaks REST API signature validation.
solution: Open outbound UDP port 123 on the Data Box device to allow NTP sync with time.windows.com. Once device time is synced, authentication succeeds.

symptom: Data Box endpoint not resolved - getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND or remote name could not be resolved
rootCause: Data Box blob endpoint name (accountname.blob.serialnumber.microsoftdatabox.com) is not added to client hosts file.
solution: Add the endpoint name to hosts file: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts (Windows) or /etc/hosts (Linux).

symptom: SSL/TLS certificate verification fails (CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED) when copying data to Data Box via AzCopy or Python SDK
rootCause: Data Box uses a self-signed TLS certificate that is not trusted by the client certificate store.
solution: Download the certificate from Azure portal, then import into system certificate store. For Python, set REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE env variable to the certificate path.

symptom: Data Box Disk upload fails with 409 error for blobs in WORM (immutable) storage container
rootCause: Blob storage container is configured as Write Once Read Many (WORM). Re-upload of existing blobs to immutable containers is blocked.
solution: Non-retryable error. Ensure listed blobs are not part of an immutable storage container. Create a new import order after resolving data configuration.

symptom: Data Box Disk managed disk conversion fails - blob size is invalid, supported sizes are between 20MB and 8192 GiB
rootCause: Page blob size is outside the 20,971,520 bytes to 8,192 GiB range required for managed disk conversion.
solution: Ensure each VHD file is between 20 MB and 8192 GiB before creating a new import order. This is a non-retryable error.

symptom: Data Box Disk upload halts with retryable error - large file shares not enabled on storage account
rootCause: Storage account does not have large file shares enabled but the data requires shares exceeding 5 TiB.
solution: Enable large file shares on the storage account in Azure portal, then confirm to resume data copy on the Data Box Disk order.

symptom: Cannot create NVMe VM: The selected image is not supported for NVMe error, or VM created as SCSI instead of NVMe with lower performance.
rootCause: NVMe VMs require Generation 2 VM image tagged as NVMe-capable. Gen1 images not supported. Untagged image silently creates SCSI-based VM. VMs with Trusted Launch cannot convert SCSI to NVMe.
solution: 1) Use Gen2 image tagged as NVMe. 2) Check supported images at enable-nvme-interface doc. 3) In portal use Disk controller filter NVMe. 4) Trusted Launch VMs must be recreated.

symptom: Creating DiskEncryptionSet fails with Cannot find the Active Directory object error when granting Key Vault access policy.
rootCause: After creating DiskEncryptionSet, Azure needs minutes to create managed identity in Entra ID. Identity not yet propagated when access policy runs.
solution: Wait a few minutes and retry. DES identity needs Get/WrapKey/UnwrapKey on Key Vault. Use az disk-encryption-set show to get principalId. Key Vault must have soft-delete and purge protection.

symptom: CMK encryption stops working after moving subscription/RG/disk to different Entra tenant. VMs fail to boot or disk I/O fails.
rootCause: CMK relies on managed identities. Cross-tenant move does not transfer managed identity. DiskEncryptionSet loses Key Vault access.
solution: Reassign managed identity to DES. Grant new identity permissions in Key Vault. DES/disks/snapshots must be same subscription/region. Key Vault can differ subscription but same region.

symptom: Cannot disable CMK on managed disk with incremental snapshots. Reverting to platform-managed key blocked.
rootCause: Platform restriction: once CMK enabled on disk with incremental snapshots, cannot be disabled. All incremental snapshots must share same DiskEncryptionSet.
solution: Copy all data to different managed disk not using CMK via az disk create --source or New-AzDiskConfig -CreateOption Copy.

symptom: Disk upload/download fails with permission denied when Entra ID data access auth enforced. Cannot generate SAS or upload VHD.
rootCause: dataAccessAuthMode=AzureActiveDirectory requires Data Operator for managed disks RBAC role with Compute/disks/download|upload/action permissions.
solution: Assign Data Operator for managed disks role. For snapshots also need snapshots/download|upload/action. VHDs cannot upload to empty snapshots. Backup/Site Recovery unsupported with Entra-secured disks.

symptom: Disk/snapshot SAS URI returns 403 after Feb 2025 change. SAS with >60 day expiration fails unexpectedly.
rootCause: Since Feb 15 2025, Azure enforces max 60-day SAS expiration for disks/snapshots. Existing SAS with longer expiry fails 60 days after creation.
solution: Revoke existing SAS via az disk revoke-access. Generate new SAS <=60 days (5184000 seconds). Update automation scripts.

symptom: Linux VM no-boot after VFAT disabled by hardening tools. Failed to mount /boot/efi or ADE VM shows mount unknown filesystem type vfat, cannot access BEK volume.
rootCause: VFAT needed for /boot/efi on Gen2 UEFI VMs and for BEK VOLUME with ADE keys. Hardening tools add install vfat /bin/true to /etc/modprobe.d/.
solution: grep -nr vfat /etc/modprobe.d/ to find config. Remove install vfat /bin/true. Regenerate initramfs (dracut -f or mkinitramfs). For encrypted OS use az vm repair. Exclude VFAT from hardening.
